title: Integrate manifest into prompt builder (prepend automatically; record reason include_impl)
description:
## Objective
Prepend the prompt manifest to the final prompt automatically (not workflow-template controlled) so every run has explicit document binding and provenance.

## Background / Rationale
We want to eliminate "did the model actually read the right docs?" ambiguity. A manifest:
- enumerates files included
- provides hashes/sizes
- gives downstream checks a stable anchor (ACK policy, ledger, metrics)

To be reliable, this must be part of prompt assembly, not something users remember to add.

## Scope
- Determine the expected file list for a run (readme/spec/optional impl), including:
  - inclusion reason (required vs optional vs impl_every_n)
  - bytes + sha256
- Compute/render the manifest using shared helpers (bd-phj).
- Prepend manifest to the prompt for:
  - `apr run` (human)
  - `apr run --dry-run` / render modes
  - `apr robot validate` and `apr robot run`
  - queue runner path
- Ensure prompt QC runs on the final assembled prompt (manifest + template), not just the raw template.
- Ensure the ledger records:
  - manifest data (or hash)
  - prompt hash of the final assembled prompt

## Acceptance Criteria
- Every run includes a manifest without users editing templates.
- `apr render`/dry-run shows the exact prompt that would be sent (including manifest).

## Test Coverage
- Covered by manifest tests + mock E2E (bd-2bq, bd-1oh, bd-2tj).
close_reason: apr's build_revision_prompt now prepends a deterministic [APR Manifest] preamble via lib/manifest.sh. 12 new tests covering inclusion reasons, determinism, and APR_NO_MANIFEST opt-out. apr render --show-manifest CLI is bd-2bq's remit.

title: Unit tests (BATS): prompt QC + lint validation + doc size policy
description:
## Objective
Add unit tests that guarantee APR never regresses on prompt QC and lint validation.

## Background / Rationale
The placeholder leak incident must never happen again. These unit tests are the safety net that prevents "obviously wrong prompt" failures from reaching ChatGPT.

## Scope
- BATS unit tests for:
  - `prompt_quality_check` rejects mustache placeholders (`{{` / `}}`) by default
  - prompt QC detects other high-signal "template not filled" markers (TODO/TBD/REPLACE_ME, etc.)
  - strict mode turns QC warnings into fatal errors (`APR_FAIL_ON_WARN=1`)
  - doc size policy behavior (warn vs fail)
  - lint/robot validation returns stable robot `.code` values and stable data shapes
- Include at least one explicit test that demonstrates the escape hatch:
  - `APR_ALLOW_CURLY_PLACEHOLDERS=1` bypasses mustache guard

## Logging requirements
- Each test writes a log file under `tests/logs/unit/`:
  - timestamp + test name
  - captured stdout/stderr
  - relevant env vars (APR_FAIL_ON_WARN, APR_ALLOW_CURLY_PLACEHOLDERS)

## Acceptance Criteria
- Tests fail if any placeholder leak class could reach ChatGPT.
- Tests fail if error output stops being actionable (missing line hits / missing remediation text).

## Dependencies
- Depends on automated_plan_reviser_pro-ufc.
- Depends on fixture migration so default fixtures don’t contain placeholders.

title: Safer operator UX: interactive confirm showing resolved prompt+manifest before launch
description:
## Objective
Add an optional interactive confirmation step that shows the *exact resolved prompt bundle* (including manifest + any trims/redactions) and requires explicit approval before invoking Oracle.

## Background / Rationale
APR runs are expensive. A 3-second manual "looks right" gate can prevent a multi-minute wasted run (e.g. unexpanded placeholders, wrong workflow, wrong round, missing files).

This is an operator-safety feature: it should never block robot mode and should be easy to disable for high-throughput users.

## Scope
### 1) Opt-in / safe defaults
- Provide an explicit way to enable confirm:
  - flag (e.g. `--confirm`)
  - and/or config/env default for interactive TTY use
- Provide a deterministic way to disable confirm even in interactive mode:
  - `--no-confirm` or `APR_NO_CONFIRM=1`
- Robot mode MUST never block on interactive input.

### 2) What the confirm screen shows (must be useful, not noisy)
Show a compact, high-signal summary:
- workflow name + round + slug
- model + engine (browser/api) + remote host (if any)
- include_impl and other key toggles
- file list (README/SPEC/IMPL) with sizes + sha256 (manifest excerpt)
- prompt size summary (bytes; and whether trimming/splitting/redaction is enabled)

Then show a preview of the resolved prompt:
- first N lines (configurable)
- and a short "tail" excerpt if helpful

### 3) Placement in the pipeline
- Confirm must happen *after*:
  - config resolution
  - lint + prompt QC
  - template expansion (if enabled)
  - redaction/trim decisions (if enabled)
- Confirm must happen *before* starting Oracle and before acquiring any long-lived locks.

### 4) Ledger/metrics integration
- Record:
  - confirm_enabled
  - user_confirmed=true/false
  - timestamp of confirmation

## Acceptance Criteria
- In interactive mode, users can catch obvious mistakes before Oracle starts.
- In robot mode, confirm is never interactive and never blocks.
- The confirm summary reflects the actual prompt that would be sent.

## Test Coverage
- Covered by hardening extras tests (bd-2pu):
  - confirm prompts in TTY-like mode (mock input)
  - no blocking in robot mode
  - preview includes manifest + expected high-signal fields

## Notes
- If gum is available, use gum confirm/pager; otherwise use `read -r`.
- Be careful not to leak secrets in the confirm UI (respect redaction mode).

title: Add apr doctor command (local+remote health checks with actionable remediation)
description:
## Objective
Add a `apr doctor` command that diagnoses the most common environmental/operational failures (especially Oracle remote mode) *before* you waste time on a run.

## Background / Rationale
In practice, the failure surface is broad:
- Oracle not installed / npx fallback not available
- Remote host not reachable / port closed
- Token mismatch
- oracle serve is busy (single-flight)
- gum present/absent changes UX paths
- Oracle version/flag mismatches (local vs remote)

Users need a single command that produces a crisp, actionable diagnosis.

## Scope
- Implement `apr doctor` (human-readable output on stderr):
  - Check: bash version, required executables, jq/shellcheck/bats (optional)
  - Check: Oracle availability (oracle or npx oracle)
  - Check: Oracle version and required flag support (best-effort):
    - ensure the flags APR uses are likely supported (e.g. browser engine, attachments mode, write-output)
  - Check: remote mode env vars when set (ORACLE_REMOTE_HOST/TOKEN)
  - Check: connectivity to remote host (TCP connect) with timeouts
  - Check: oracle status call (if available) to infer busy/idle
  - Check: remote oracle serve "identity" (best-effort):
    - confirm the remote is actually an oracle serve endpoint (not a random port)
    - surface obvious local/remote version mismatches
  - Print remediation suggestions (copy/paste commands)
- Add a `--json` output mode for automation.

## Acceptance Criteria
- Running `apr doctor` makes it obvious what is wrong and how to fix it.
- In remote mode, doctor can distinguish:
  - unreachable host
  - reachable but busy
  - reachable but auth/token failure
  - reachable but incompatible oracle (missing flags / wrong version)

## Notes
- Doctor must be safe: no destructive operations.
close_reason: Implemented apr doctor operational diagnostics via premortem check: local tool/oracle/npx/status/remote host+token/connectivity JSON, with integration coverage

title: APR Hardening Extras: Confirm UX + Model Policy + Redaction + Secret Scan
description:
## Objective
Track optional safety/quality enhancements that improve operator experience and reduce accidental low-quality runs, without blocking the core hardening milestone.

## Background / Rationale
Core hardening is: lint + prompt QC + doc policy + doctor + schema explain + stable error codes.

Beyond that, there are helpful extras:
- Optional interactive confirmations before expensive runs
- Policy nudges for model/engine settings (extended reasoning)
- Opt-in redaction layer to prevent accidental secret leakage
- Heuristic secret scanning to warn/fail-fast when likely secrets are present

These are valuable, but should not be required to ship the core safety upgrade.

## Scope
- Confirm UX for interactive use
- Model/engine policy checks
- Optional redaction layer
- Optional secret scanning (warn; strict fail)

## Acceptance Criteria
- Extras are discoverable and well documented.
- Extras remain opt-in / overrideable so they do not block power users.

title: Diagnostic bundle: capture prompt/config/ledger/logs (manual + on-failure)
description:
## Objective
Create a deterministic diagnostic bundle for each run that captures everything needed to debug or share the run (without relying on terminal scrollback).

## Background / Rationale
APR failures are often expensive (time + attention). When something goes wrong, the key question is: "what *exactly* was sent, with what config, and what happened?"

A diagnostic bundle also makes it easy to:
- file bugs with complete reproduction context
- hand the bundle to another model to review
- compare two runs side-by-side

## Scope
- Add a bundle writer that can write a directory like:
  - `.apr/bundles/<workflow>/<round>/<run_id>/`
  - or `.apr/bundles/<run_id>/` (final path TDB; document it)
- Bundle contents (minimum):
  - `prompt.txt` (exact rendered prompt, incl. manifest)
  - `manifest.json` (machine-readable file list with sha/bytes)
  - `config.resolved.json` (resolved workflow/config snapshot)
  - `oracle.args.json` (sanitized args: engine/model/slug/remote host; redact token)
  - `stderr.log` / `stdout.log` (captured from apr invocation where possible)
  - `result.meta.json` (code, exit_code, start/end timestamps, durations)
  - `ledger.meta.json` (copy or link to run ledger artifact, if enabled)
- Triggers:
  - Manual: `apr bundle <run_id>` (or `apr render --bundle`)
  - Automatic: `apr run` writes bundle on failure when `APR_BUNDLE_ON_FAIL=1`
- Redaction:
  - redact tokens/secrets from args and env dumps
  - never include browser cookies or any oracle internal sensitive files

## Acceptance Criteria
- Given only the bundle directory, an operator can understand:
  - which docs were included
  - which prompt was sent
  - which oracle host/model was used
  - why it failed (code + key stderr excerpts)
- Bundling does not change the behavior of successful runs unless explicitly enabled.

## Notes
- Keep it pure Bash.
- Prefer plain files over archives; optional `tar.gz` export can be added later.

title: Busy detection: define reliable signatures (stderr patterns/exit codes) + fixtures
description:
## Objective
Define how APR detects oracle "busy" (single-flight) reliably and test it with fixtures so busy handling is deterministic.

## Background / Rationale
In high-throughput use, busy is not exceptional; it is normal. If APR misclassifies busy as a generic failure, operators lose time and queues break.

Conversely, if APR misclassifies real errors as busy, it will spin/wait forever.

## Scope
- Collect busy signatures observed in the wild (stderr + exit codes), e.g.:
  - "ERROR: busy"
  - "User error (browser-automation): busy"
  - any stable oracle exit codes (if exposed)
- Implement a detection helper that:
  - returns a boolean busy
  - extracts any useful details (how long busy, which host, etc.)
  - never matches unrelated errors
- Define a small fixture corpus for unit/integration tests.
- Ensure mapping to error taxonomy (bd-3tj):
  - `BUSY` (or equivalent) is distinct from `ORACLE_ERROR` / `NETWORK_ERROR` / `CONFIG_ERROR`.

## Acceptance Criteria
- Busy detection is stable and does not misclassify other errors.
- Given fixture outputs, detection result is deterministic.

## Notes
- This bead is strictly about detection; waiting/backoff is handled in bd-3du/bd-2kd.
close_reason: Detection helper + 4 signatures + 13 fixtures + 37 tests. No false positives on busylight/busyness/network/config errors.

title: Tests: apr doctor (fixtures for oracle missing, remote unreachable, token missing, busy) with rich logs
description:
## Objective
Add tests that ensure `apr doctor` remains accurate, actionable, and stable across edge cases.

## Scope
- Unit/integration tests (BATS) that simulate:
  - oracle missing
  - npx missing
  - remote env vars missing
  - remote host unreachable (connection refused/timeout)
  - remote host reachable but busy
  - remote host reachable but auth/token failure (if oracle exposes)
  - oracle version / flag mismatch cases:
    - local oracle too old / missing expected flags
    - remote endpoint responds but does not look like oracle serve
- Logging:
  - tests/logs/doctor/<ts>/... capturing stdout/stderr and env

## Acceptance Criteria
- Doctor tests fail if doctor output becomes misleading (e.g. claims success when remote unreachable).
- When doctor detects version/flag incompatibility, it emits an explicit remediation message.

## Dependencies
- Depends on automated_plan_reviser_pro-ufc and on the doctor implementation bead.

title: Error taxonomy: stable code + exit-code mapping across lint/run/robot/queue/doctor
description:
## Objective
Make APR failures programmatically actionable and consistent by standardizing:
- robot JSON failure codes
- human exit codes
- human/robot stderr tags

## Background / Rationale
APR is used both interactively and by automation/agents. If error handling is inconsistent, scripts become brittle and humans waste time interpreting failures.

We want a single source of truth for:
- which error happened (machine-readable)
- how the process exits (exit code)
- how a human can remediate (hint)

## Canonical Output Contract
### Robot JSON envelope (existing convention)
Robot mode outputs JSON to stdout:
- `ok: boolean`
- `code: string`  (stable machine-readable code; when ok=false, identifies the failure class)
- `data: object`  (structured payload; e.g. errors[], warnings[])
- `hint?: string` (actionable remediation)
- `meta: { v: <apr version>, ts: <rfc3339> }`

### Human stderr tag (grep-friendly)
For fatal errors, APR should also emit a compact stderr tag:
- `APR_ERROR_CODE=<code>`

This tag must be:
- stable
- ASCII-only
- emitted even when `NO_COLOR=1`

## Scope
### 1) Define the canonical `code` taxonomy (stable strings)
Proposed initial set (expand carefully):
- `ok`
- `usage_error`
- `not_configured`
- `config_error`
- `validation_failed`
- `dependency_missing`
- `busy`
- `network_error`
- `update_error`
- `attachment_mismatch`
- `not_implemented`
- `internal_error`

Rules:
- codes are lowercase snake_case
- adding new codes is allowed
- renaming/removing codes requires a deliberate migration plan (tests must catch drift)

### 2) Define exit-code mapping
Exit codes should remain semantic and stable. Map categories (example mapping; finalize during implementation):
- `ok` -> 0
- `usage_error` -> 2
- `dependency_missing` -> 3
- `config_error` / `validation_failed` -> 4
- `network_error` -> 10
- `update_error` -> 11
- `busy` -> dedicated code (add a constant if needed) OR map to a documented existing code

### 3) Standardize error emission helpers
- Provide a small helper that, given:
  - `code`
  - human message
  - optional hint
  - optional structured data
  emits the correct shape for robot mode and the correct stderr/exit behavior for human mode.

### 4) Cross-command enforcement
Apply the taxonomy across the commands we’re hardening:
- lint
- run
- robot commands
- queue runner
- doctor

## Acceptance Criteria
- A wrapper script can reliably branch on `code` without string-matching human stderr.
- Exit codes are stable and documented.
- Human stderr includes `APR_ERROR_CODE=<code>` for fatal errors.

## Test Coverage
- Enforced by contract tests (bd-18r).

## Notes
- Be conservative about adding new codes.
- Prefer enriching `data` over inventing new codes.
close_reason: Verified stable error taxonomy, exit-code mapping, APR_ERROR_CODE tags, and contract tests

title: Templating UX: workflow toggle + docs + examples + migration guidance
description:
## Objective
Make safe templating *usable* and hard to misuse by providing clear docs, examples, and UX affordances that prevent placeholder leaks.

## Background / Rationale
APR recently demonstrated a high-cost failure mode: sending an unexpanded placeholder like `{{README}}` into ChatGPT via Oracle.

Core hardening now treats unexpanded placeholders as fatal by default. However, power users still legitimately want templating features (include file, include excerpt, include hash, etc.). The safe templating design (bd-2nq) introduces an allowlisted directive syntax (example: `[[APR:FILE README.md]]`) that can be expanded deterministically and then re-validated by prompt QC.

This bead is the *human-facing* layer: it ensures users can adopt templating without reading source code and without discovering footguns.

## Scope
### 1) Clear user docs (self-contained)
- Document the directive syntax + semantics (aligned with bd-2nq), including explicit examples:
  - `[[APR:FILE README.md]]` - embed file contents
  - `[[APR:SHA README.md]]` - embed sha256
  - `[[APR:SIZE README.md]]` - embed byte size
  - `[[APR:EXCERPT SPEC.md 2000]]` - embed first N bytes/chars per spec
- Explain safety invariants:
  - `{{...}}` / `}}` / other placeholder syntaxes are rejected by default
  - directives only work when templating is explicitly enabled
  - post-expansion prompt QC is always run; leftovers are fatal
- Document operator controls:
  - how to enable templating per workflow (exact config key once implemented)
  - how `apr lint` behaves when directives are present but templating is disabled (must be explicit and actionable)
  - how `apr render`/dry-run preview shows resolved output

### 2) Examples that match reality
- Provide at least 2-3 sample workflow templates (in the repo) that demonstrate:
  - a normal bundle (README+SPEC+optional impl) without directives
  - a templated bundle using `[[APR:*]]` directives
  - a migration example showing before/after conversion from `{{README}}` patterns
- Ensure examples are exercised by tests (so docs/examples cannot silently rot).

### 3) Migration guidance (no safety bypass)
- Write a migration section that maps old mustache placeholders to safe directives or safe non-templated phrasing.
- Reference the migration tool (bd-kii) once implemented:
  - it should *never* silently rewrite; it should show a diff + require explicit confirmation.

## Acceptance Criteria
- A new user can adopt safe templating end-to-end from docs alone.
- When directives are present but templating is disabled, APR fails or warns with:
  - the exact config knob to enable templating
  - a pointer to the directive docs/examples
- Docs/examples are consistent with actual behavior (no fictional flags/syntax).

## Test Coverage
- Covered indirectly by templating tests (bd-ptx) and lint/QC tests (bd-3ks) via:
  - validating example workflows
  - ensuring no `{{...}}` placeholders are used in default/example templates.

title: Optional safe redaction layer for prompts (prevent accidental secret leakage)
description:
## Objective
Provide an opt-in redaction pass that removes *high-confidence secret patterns* from the prompt bundle before anything is copied/sent to ChatGPT.

## Background / Rationale
APR often includes large blobs of repo text (README/spec/impl). It is easy to accidentally include:
- API keys
- access tokens
- private keys

A best-effort redaction mode reduces the blast radius for operators who want extra safety. This is NOT a security guarantee, but it can prevent common accidental leaks.

## Scope
### 1) Opt-in mode + UX
- Redaction is disabled by default.
- Enable via an explicit flag/env (final interface TBD), e.g.:
  - `--redact`
  - or `APR_REDACT=1`
- When enabled:
  - the *redacted* prompt is what gets:
    - sent to Oracle
    - printed by `apr render`
    - copied by `--copy` / clipboard helpers

### 2) High-signal patterns (conservative allowlist)
Redact only high-confidence patterns to avoid destroying technical docs:
- Private key blocks: `-----BEGIN ... PRIVATE KEY-----`
- Common token prefixes (examples; tune carefully):
  - OpenAI-style: `sk-...`
  - GitHub tokens: `ghp_...`, `github_pat_...`
  - Slack: `xoxb-...`, `xoxp-...`
  - AWS access key id: `AKIA...` (and similar)
- "Bearer" headers:
  - `Authorization: Bearer <...>`

### 3) Redaction semantics (deterministic, auditable)
- Replace secrets with stable sentinel strings, e.g.:
  - `<<REDACTED:OPENAI_KEY>>`
  - `<<REDACTED:PRIVATE_KEY_BLOCK>>`
- Never print the full secret to stdout/stderr.
- Prefer per-source redaction (so we can report path + line numbers):
  - scan/redact each included file independently before composing the final prompt
  - also scan/redact the workflow template text itself (label as `template`)

### 4) Reporting + controls
- Human mode:
  - print a summary: patterns matched + counts + file paths
  - do not print secrets (only redacted snippets)
- Robot mode:
  - include structured redaction metadata in JSON output
- False-positive controls:
  - allow disabling specific pattern families via config/env (documented)
  - optional allowlist file to ignore *known-safe* matches (off by default; document risks)

### 5) Ledger integration
- Record in the run ledger:
  - whether redaction was enabled
  - which pattern families matched
  - counts per file
  - best-effort line numbers

## Acceptance Criteria
- In redaction mode, obvious secret patterns do not reach ChatGPT.
- Redaction behavior is deterministic and clearly reported.
- Redaction never prints secrets to logs.

## Test Coverage
- Covered by hardening extras tests (bd-2pu):
  - verify secrets are replaced by sentinel strings
  - verify logs/bundles do not contain the raw secret

## Notes
- This is a safety valve, not a substitute for good repo hygiene.
close_reason: lib/redact.sh + 18 tests cover the 7 documented pattern classes with conservative prefix anchors. Typed sentinels (<<REDACTED:TYPE>>) match the bead's auditable-substitution requirement. Per-call counter reset + JSON summary ready for ledger surfacing via APR_LEDGER_TRUST_JSON. apr-side --redact flag + APR_REDACT=1 wiring tracked as follow-on.

title: Integration tests (BATS): oracle busy handling + queue runner behavior
description:
## Objective
Add integration tests for the scheduler-like behavior: busy handling, queue sequencing, locks, and idempotency.

## Background / Rationale
Busy/queue features are where APR reliability is won or lost. These tests should verify correct behavior at the command level (CLI integration), not just helper functions.

## Scope
- Use fixtures / a deterministic mock oracle (no real network/browser).
- Test cases:
  - busy -> backoff/wait path produces clear progress messaging and eventually proceeds
  - enqueue many -> sequential run ordering is deterministic
  - lock held -> second runner exits with structured error (no corruption)
  - idempotency: output exists -> skip/refuse/attach according to flags
  - error contract: correct robot `.code` and exit code on failure paths

## Logging requirements
- Each integration test writes a log bundle under `tests/logs/integration/`:
  - timestamp + test name
  - env vars
  - captured stdout/stderr
  - any generated queue/round/ledger artifacts

## Acceptance Criteria
- Behaviors are stable across Linux/macOS.
- Failures are debuggable from logs alone.

## Dependencies
- Depends on automated_plan_reviser_pro-ufc.
- Depends on implementation beads for busy/queue/idempotency.
- Depends on fixture migration so defaults do not contain placeholders.

title: Metrics vNext: execution signals (busy/retries/queue outcomes)
description:
## Objective
Extend APR analytics/metrics to surface execution/runtime signals from busy handling, retries, and queue processing.

## Background / Rationale
Even when prompts are correct, runs can be low-quality or low-trust due to execution issues:
- oracle busy waits
- retry storms
- queue runner failures

If these signals are not surfaced, users must read raw logs and will miss important failures.

## Scope
- Extend metrics schema (versioned) to include execution signals:
  - busy_wait_ms, busy_wait_events
  - retry_attempts, retry_exit_codes
  - queue metadata (queued_at, started_at, completed_at, queue_run_id)
  - outcome classification (success/busy_timeout/network_error/etc) aligned with error taxonomy
- Update:
  - metrics writers (run + robot + queue runner)
  - backfill logic where possible
  - stats/dashboard rendering to show runtime warnings prominently
  - exports (json/csv/md) to include new fields

## Acceptance Criteria
- A user can tell from `apr stats`/dashboard when a round is low-trust due to execution problems (busy timeouts, repeated retries, queue failures).

## Notes
- Coordinate with `bd-3tj` error taxonomy and queue/busy implementation beads.

title: apr lint CLI: command plumbing, help text, exit codes, and JSON output mode
description:
## Objective
Implement the user-facing `apr lint` command as the canonical validation entry point (human + robot).

## Background / Rationale
APR already has preflight checks and a robot validate path, but validation is not a cohesive, explicit workflow step.

The placeholder leak incident demonstrates validation must be:
- first-class
- user-visible
- a default gate before expensive oracle runs

## Scope
### Command Surface
- `apr lint [--workflow <name>] [--round <N>] [--include-impl]`
  - Runs the shared validation pipeline (bd-30c).
  - Emits human-readable results to stderr.
  - Exit codes are stable and documented.
- `apr robot lint` (or improved `apr robot validate` alias):
  - Emits machine-readable JSON per existing robot conventions.
  - Includes `errors[]` and `warnings[]` arrays.

### Output Requirements
- Human output:
  - includes file paths
  - includes remediation hints
  - includes a short machine-readable error tag (bd-3tj)
- JSON output:
  - stable `code` values
  - stable data shape

### Exit Code Policy (draft)
- 0: ok
- non-zero: validation failure
- strict mode can treat warnings as errors (aligned with bd-zd6 / bd-1gl)

## Acceptance Criteria
- `apr lint` can run in CI on a repo without oracle.
- `apr run` can delegate to `apr lint` for gating.

## Notes
- Keep APR pure Bash.
- Do not duplicate validation logic; this command is a wrapper around the shared core.
close_reason: Implemented apr lint and robot lint CLI plumbing with shared validation output, help text, exit codes, and no-Oracle smoke coverage.

title: Oracle remote pool support (choose first idle host; multi-mac-mini)
description:
## Objective
Support multiple `oracle serve` hosts (e.g. multiple Mac minis) and automatically select a reachable, non-busy host to increase throughput and reduce "busy" waits.

## Background / Rationale
In real usage, a single oracle-remote host is a throughput bottleneck because browser runs are often single-flight. Users may have multiple machines available (each running `oracle serve`). APR should be able to spread load transparently.

Oracle already supports delegation via:
- `--remote-host <host:port>`
- `--remote-token <token>`

This bead makes APR able to:
- choose a host automatically
- fail fast when all hosts are unreachable
- integrate with busy handling + queue runner

## Scope
### 1) Configuration format
Support a pool specification (final interface TBD):
- Env-based (simple):
  - `ORACLE_REMOTE_POOL="host1:9333,host2:9333"`
  - token via:
    - `ORACLE_REMOTE_TOKEN` (shared)
    - or `ORACLE_REMOTE_TOKENS="host1:tokenA,host2:tokenB"`
- Optional workflow-level config (future): per-workflow pool override.

### 2) Selection policy (deterministic, debuggable)
- Default policy:
  - stable order traversal with optional round-robin starting index
  - choose the first host that is:
    - reachable
    - and (best-effort) appears idle
- Track per-host last-failure timestamps to avoid repeatedly hammering a dead host.
- On selection, print a clear log line:
  - `Selected oracle remote host: host2:9333 (reason=idle)`

### 3) Health + busy probing (best-effort)
- Reachability:
  - TCP connect check (short timeout)
  - if possible, lightweight remote status probe
- Busy probing:
  - best-effort: query remote host for busy/idle if oracle serve exposes a status endpoint
  - fallback: optimistic selection; if the run returns busy, delegate to the existing busy handling logic

### 4) Integration with run/queue
- `apr run` (when remote pool configured):
  - inject Oracle flags `--remote-host` and `--remote-token` for the chosen host
- `apr queue run`:
  - each dequeued entry should select a host at execution time
- Ledger/metrics (if enabled):
  - record selected remote host, pool list, and any fallback attempts

### 5) Failure behavior
- If all hosts unreachable:
  - fail with a stable `.code` + actionable remediation:
    - "start oracle serve on one of these hosts"
    - "check token"
    - "check firewall/VPN"
- If hosts are reachable but busy:
  - respect busy policy (wait vs enqueue vs fail) rather than inventing new behavior.

## Acceptance Criteria
- With a pool configured, APR can automatically use a non-busy host when available.
- When one host is down/busy, APR either selects another host or cleanly falls back to busy wait.

## Test Coverage
- Covered by execution extras tests (bd-2y8):
  - unreachable host(s)
  - busy host selection fallback
  - token mismatch failure

## Notes
- This does not introduce a new server; it only orchestrates existing `oracle serve` instances.

title: Implement safe template expansion + post-expansion QC (guarantee zero leftovers)
description:
## Objective
Implement the safe template directive system and guarantee no placeholder leaks by re-running prompt QC after expansion.

## Background / Rationale
If templating exists, it must be safe-by-construction. The core invariant is:
- After expansion, the final prompt must pass prompt_quality_check.

## Scope
- Implement directive parsing and expansion per the design bead.
- Add workflow setting to enable templating (off by default).
- Re-run prompt QC after expansion and fail if any placeholders remain.
- Record expansion activity in the run ledger (counts, which directives used).

## Acceptance Criteria
- When templating is enabled, common directives expand correctly.
- If a directive fails to expand, APR errors with actionable info (file/path/line).
- No placeholder syntax leaks into ChatGPT.

## Notes
- Must be deterministic and fast (pure Bash string ops).
close_reason: apr's build_revision_prompt now runs lib/template.sh's directive engine when workflow yaml sets template_directives.enabled=true (or template_directives_enabled: true). Path safety (allow_traversal / allow_absolute) is opt-in via yaml. Engine output captured via tempfile so APR_TEMPLATE_ERROR_* globals survive — failures emit actionable [apr] template: stderr context and rc=1, which the lint gate (bd-35i) turns into a refusal to invoke Oracle. Post-expansion QC was already wired via bd-2lc / lint_collect_findings. 9 new tests. Ledger recording deferred to bd-1mt.

title: Workflow migration tool: detect old {{README}} templates and auto-convert to safe directives
description:
## Objective
Provide a workflow-migration helper that upgrades older prompt templates that use unsafe placeholder patterns (e.g. `{{README}}`) into the new safe directive system (bd-2nq), without requiring manual editing.

## Background / Rationale
APR now treats unexpanded placeholders (like `{{README}}`) as fatal because they waste expensive Oracle runs.

Existing user workflows (and older examples) may still contain these placeholder patterns. A migration tool reduces friction and prevents users from "turning off safety" just to keep going.

## Scope
### 1) Command surface
Add a command like:
- `apr workflow migrate [--workflow <name>|--all]`

### 2) What it detects
Scan workflow YAML(s) and detect high-signal unsafe placeholders in template blocks:
- `{{README}}`, `{{SPEC}}`, `{{IMPL}}`
- common variants (whitespace, lowercase)
- optionally other placeholder syntaxes if they show up in practice (keep allowlist conservative)

### 3) Migration mapping (deterministic)
Convert unsafe placeholders to safe directives (bd-2nq) where possible:
- `{{README}}` -> `[[APR:FILE README.md]]` (or the workflow's configured readme path)
- `{{SPEC}}`   -> `[[APR:FILE SPEC.md]]`
- `{{IMPL}}`   -> `[[APR:FILE <impl-path>]]` (only if impl configured)

If an exact mapping is ambiguous:
- do not guess silently
- emit an instructive message and require manual resolution

### 4) No silent writes (safety invariant)
- Default behavior is preview-only:
  - show a unified diff of proposed changes
  - show which workflows would be modified
- Apply behavior requires explicit confirmation:
  - interactive prompt (TTY) or a `--yes` flag

### 5) Post-migration validation
After applying changes (or in preview mode), the tool should:
- run `apr lint` on the affected workflow(s)
- ensure placeholder leak guards no longer trigger

## Acceptance Criteria
- Users can upgrade old workflows without manual editing.
- The tool never silently rewrites files.
- The migrated workflow passes lint with templating enabled (or clearly instructs how to enable it).

## Dependencies
- Depends on templating implementation (bd-btu) so generated directives actually work.

## Notes
- This tool should make it *easier* to stay safe, not easier to bypass safety.

title: Workflow schema validation + apr workflow explain (resolved config + template selection)
description:
## Objective
Reduce "mystery behavior" by validating workflow YAML keys and exposing a command that prints the resolved, effective configuration for a given workflow/round.

## Background / Rationale
APR workflow YAML has enough options (docs paths, model, output dir, impl_every_n, templates, thinking_time, oracle settings) that misconfiguration is easy. Users need a quick way to answer:
- Which template will be used this round (with/without impl)?
- Which files will be included?
- Which model/engine flags will be used?

## Scope
- Add schema-ish validation:
  - Required keys present
  - Unknown/unexpected keys detected (warning by default)
  - Better missing-key messages
- Add `apr workflow explain [--workflow X] [--round N]`:
  - Print resolved paths (readme/spec/impl)
  - Print computed include_impl decision (impl_every_n + flags)
  - Print model + thinking_time + engine policy
  - Print output slug and output path

## Acceptance Criteria
- A user can run `apr workflow explain` and understand exactly what `apr run` will do without reading the code.

## Notes
- Best-effort line numbers are OK; do not build a full YAML parser.
close_reason: Implemented apr_lib_validate_workflow_schema in lib/validate.sh to validate required keys and warn on unknown ones. Added apr workflow explain command for resolved path and decision introspection. Added BATS tests.

title: APR vNext (Full): Core + Templating + Extras
description:
## Objective
Track the *full* set of APR improvements discussed (core reliability + optional advanced capabilities) as one umbrella deliverable.

## Background / Rationale
The core milestone (bd-3hi) delivers the must-have safety/provenance/queue behavior. Additional optional epics (templating, remote pool, confirm UX, etc.) are valuable but should not block core shipping.

This umbrella epic exists so we can still say "everything from the plan is done" when the optional epics/extras are complete.

## Scope
- Core vNext milestone: bd-3hi
- Safe templating + migration: bd-2mc
- Execution extras (pool + oversize prompt handling): bd-2no
- Hardening extras (confirm + model policy + redaction + secret scan): bd-3ow
- Operational extras (diagnostic bundle + event log + perf caching): bd-1lg
- Live oracle-remote smoke test script (operator-run; not CI): bd-3a4

## Acceptance Criteria
- All included epics/tasks are closed.

dependencies.metadata: {"rationale":"Existing APR vNext Full umbrella must include the v18 multi-provider planning engine rather than remaining limited to the older queue/manifest/templating plan."}

title: Manifest internals: file hashing/size helpers + compact manifest formatter
description:
## Objective
Implement deterministic helpers for computing document metadata and rendering the prompt manifest section.

## Background / Rationale
The manifest is the "binding" mechanism between the prompt and the documents:
- tells the model which files are included
- provides hashes/sizes for provenance
- allows downstream checks (ACK, files-report verification)

For it to be useful, it must be stable and reproducible.

## Scope
### Helper Functions
- Compute sha256 for a file (content hash)
- Compute byte size (exact)
- Normalize basename/path rendering (avoid confusing absolute paths)
- Determine inclusion reason (required vs optional vs impl_every_n)

### Formatting
- Compact, predictable ordering (stable sort)
- Stable whitespace (diff-friendly)
- Support both human-facing text manifest and machine-readable JSON representation (if needed by ledger/metrics)

### Edge Cases
- Missing/optional implementation file:
  - record as "skipped" with reason
- Non-UTF8 content:
  - treat as bytes; do not attempt to decode

## Acceptance Criteria
- Given the same inputs, manifest output is byte-identical.
- Manifest ordering does not change across runs.

## Test Coverage
- Covered by prompt-manifest and artifact tests (bd-2bq, bd-1oh).
close_reason: lib/manifest.sh with 39 passing unit tests. Helpers emit byte-deterministic output matching run-ledger.schema.json files[] shape.

title: Tests (BATS): safe templating directives (expand/correctness/no-leak) with rich logs
description:
## Objective
Add comprehensive tests for the safe templating/directive system so it is trustworthy and cannot regress into placeholder leaks.

## Background / Rationale
Safe templating is an advanced feature that can easily become a footgun if not heavily tested. The entire point is: templating must be safe-by-construction.

## Scope
- Unit tests for:
  - parsing directive tokens
  - directive expansion for FILE/SHA/SIZE/EXCERPT
  - failure modes (missing file, invalid args) produce actionable errors
  - post-expansion prompt QC runs and fails on leftovers
  - mustache placeholders remain rejected by default
- Integration test:
  - a workflow with templating enabled expands and passes lint, producing a deterministic prompt bundle

## Logging
- Write timestamped logs under `tests/logs/templating/` including stdout/stderr and env.

## Acceptance Criteria
- It is impossible to send `{{README}}` (or similar placeholder) to ChatGPT even with templating enabled.

## Dependencies
- Depends on automated_plan_reviser_pro-ufc and the templating implementation beads.

title: Prompt size estimator + trimming strategy (avoid ChatGPT size limits deterministically)
description:
## Objective
Estimate the *final resolved prompt* size before invoking Oracle and apply a deterministic, explicitly-logged strategy when the bundle is too large for the ChatGPT UI.

## Background / Rationale
APR workflows can bundle very large READMEs/specs/impl files. ChatGPT UI + browser automation have practical size limits. When a run fails due to oversize payloads, it is expensive and confusing.

We want deterministic behavior:
- detect oversize risk early
- fail fast (strict) or trim predictably (auto-trim)
- always make trimming visible to the model and to the operator (manifest + ledger)

## Scope
### 1) Size estimation (ground truth = bytes of the outgoing prompt)
- Compute byte size of the *exact prompt string* that would be passed to Oracle (`-p`), including:
  - manifest preamble
  - rendered template content
  - any included docs
  - any directive expansions (if templating is enabled)
- Provide a stable output surface:
  - human: stderr summary (bytes per component + total)
  - robot: JSON fields (total_bytes, per_file_bytes)

### 2) Policy + thresholds
- Introduce explicit budgets (defaults should be conservative):
  - global max bytes: e.g. `APR_MAX_PROMPT_BYTES` (name TBD)
  - optional per-file caps (README/SPEC/IMPL)
- Modes:
  - `warn-only`: log a warning, continue
  - `strict`: refuse to invoke Oracle when over budget
  - `auto-trim`: deterministically truncate (see below)

### 3) Deterministic trimming strategy (auto-trim)
- Trimming MUST be explicit in the prompt using unambiguous markers (so the model knows it is partial):
  - e.g. `--- TRUNCATED: SPEC.md (kept first 20000 bytes of 180233) ---`
- Trimming MUST be recorded in ledger + metrics:
  - original size, kept size, policy used, and a hash of the *original* content
- Default trim order should preserve the highest-value context:
  - attempt: drop or trim IMPL first (since it is optional / often large)
  - then trim SPEC
  - then trim README last
  - never trim the manifest/instructions
- Ensure trimming is stable across runs given the same inputs (no randomness).

## Acceptance Criteria
- Operators can predict whether a run will fit (before Oracle starts).
- In strict mode, APR never starts Oracle if the prompt exceeds the configured budget.
- In auto-trim mode, APR produces a prompt that is:
  - within budget
  - clearly marked as truncated
  - fully auditable via ledger/metrics

## Test Coverage
- Covered by execution extras tests (bd-2y8):
  - size accounting correctness
  - trim ordering
  - explicit markers present
  - ledger records trimming decisions

## Notes
- Treat token estimation as optional; byte-exact estimation is the invariant.
close_reason: lib/size.sh + 25 tests deliver the estimation + classification primitives (total/breakdown/check_budget/policy_resolve). Conservative defaults from AGENTS.md (~200KB inline-paste threshold). apr-side wiring (warn/strict/auto-trim enforcement, --max-prompt-bytes flag, manifest 'trim' field per bd-246) is the natural follow-up; tracked in beads for execution-extras (bd-2y8 already covers the trim test side).

title: Add apr lint command (human + robot) and wire it into apr run
description:
## Objective
Introduce a first-class lint/validation command that validates workflows, documents, and prompt templates *before* Oracle is invoked.

## Background / Rationale
APR already has robot_validate and some preflight checks, but they are not a cohesive, first-class workflow step. The placeholder leak incident demonstrates that validation must be an explicit, user-facing feature and a default gate.

## Scope
- Add `apr lint [--workflow X] [--round N]`:
  - Human output on stderr (clear, actionable)
  - Non-zero exit code on fatal errors
- Add `apr robot lint` (or alias `apr robot validate` improvements) with stable JSON error codes
- Ensure lint includes prompt QC checks for both templates (with and without impl)

## Deliverables
- New command(s) + help text + README docs
- Stable exit codes for CI usage
- Deterministic output formatting (easy to grep)

## Acceptance Criteria
- Running `apr lint` on a broken workflow fails fast with an error that includes file paths and remediation
- `apr run` refuses to start Oracle when `apr lint` fails (unless explicitly bypassed)

## Notes
- Keep APR pure Bash.
- Reuse existing validation logic; refactor only if it reduces duplication.
close_reason:
Scope subsumed by predecessors:
- 'apr lint' human + robot command -> shipped via bd-9fl (apr lint CLI plumbing + lint_collect_findings).
- 'apr run refuses to invoke Oracle on lint failure' -> shipped via bd-35i (run_lint_gate + robot_lint_gate + --no-lint bypass).
- Validation logic + stable exit codes -> shipped via bd-30c (lib/validate.sh + apr_lib_validate_*).
- Prompt QC for both templates (with/without impl) -> implemented in lint_collect_findings which calls build_revision_prompt for both modes and runs apr_lib_validate_prompt_qc on each.
- Help text + readme entries -> bd-9fl added gum + plain + robot help, plus --fail-on-warn (bd-35i).
Test coverage: tests/unit/test_lint_gate.bats (11 tests) + test_validate.bats (52 tests).

title: Document content policy: min-bytes thresholds, suspiciously-small warnings, and strict mode
description:
## Objective
Make the "empty/suspiciously-small docs" checks configurable and enforceable as fatal in strict mode.

## Background / Rationale
Preflight currently fails on empty files and warns on files < 64 bytes. That is a good start, but we want a policy that is:
- Per-document type (README/spec/impl) because expected sizes differ
- Reusable in lint/robot modes
- Escapable explicitly (for minimal toy workflows)

## Scope
- Add a policy layer for docs:
  - Required docs must be non-empty
  - Per-doc warning threshold (bytes)
  - Optional per-doc fatal threshold (bytes) in strict mode
- Expose strictness:
  - CLI flag: `--fail-on-warn`
  - Env var: `APR_FAIL_ON_WARN=1`
- Ensure errors mention the file path and current size.

## Acceptance Criteria
- Running in strict mode refuses to start Oracle if doc sizes are below policy
- Default mode preserves current behavior (warn, not fail)

## Notes
- Keep logic centralized so `apr lint`, `apr run`, and `apr robot validate/run` behave consistently.
close_reason: lib/validate.sh extended with apr_lib_validate_doc_size + doc_sizes batch wrapper. Per-role defaults (readme=256, spec=1024, impl=512). APR_DOC_<ROLE>_WARN_BYTES / APR_DOC_<ROLE>_FATAL_BYTES env overrides. Fatal beats warn. Strict mode promotion via finalize_strict. 11 new tests (63 total in test_validate.bats). apr wiring follow-on as needed.

title: APR Execution Robustness: Busy Handling + Queue + Idempotency
description:
## Objective
Make APR behave like a reliable scheduler/orchestrator rather than a thin wrapper around oracle, especially in oracle-remote single-flight environments.

## Background / Rationale
In practice, oracle serve on a Mac mini often behaves as single-flight. Concurrent runs return ERROR: busy. Today this is a sharp edge because it causes wasted manual effort and brittle automation.

We want APR to support both:
- Operator-style interactive use (clear messaging, attach suggestions)
- Automation-style usage (queue, backoff, stable error codes)

## Scope (core)
- Detect and handle oracle busy with backoff + status polling
- Persistent per-workflow queue (enqueue many rounds; process sequentially)
- Idempotency guardrails (attach if slug already running; avoid duplicate work)
- Metrics surfaced for busy/queue outcomes so users can trust automation

## Explicitly NOT required to close this epic (tracked separately)
- Remote oracle pool selection (bd-2no)
- Oversize prompt handling: size estimation / trimming / split-bundle (bd-2no)

## Acceptance Criteria
- A user can start many runs quickly without babysitting; APR serializes/queues appropriately.
- Busy/lock failures are explained with actionable next steps (wait/attach/queue).
- Queue runner behavior is deterministic and leaves a provenance trail (ledger + metrics).
